What does §1.1 (The Container Revolution) of Container Vessel Stowage Planning explain?

Section 1.1 "The Container Revolution" (Chapter 1, Introduction) explains: The container revolution started 26 April 1956, when Malcolm McLean refitted a T-2 tanker called SS Ideal-X and carried 58 containers from Newark, New Jersey to Houston. Before that date, individual cargo items were loaded and unloaded manually by dockworkers using inefficeint work processes that could take more than 50% of the time and cost of an ocean voyage (see Figure 1.1). Various container systems were used before 1956, but Malcolm McLean with his trucking background was the first to understand that the shipping industry’s business was to move cargo and not sailing ships. Section 1.2 "Liner Shipping Business" (Chapter 1, Introduction) explains: A liner service is a fleet of container vessels with a common management that provides a fixed schedule of calls at regular time intervals between named ports. The difference between a liner service and a tramp service is that the liner shipping companies accept cargo from all customers and sail according to a published schedule, whether the vessels are filled or not . What does §1.2.4 (Internal Communication) of Container Vessel Stowage Planning explain?

Section 1.2.4 "Internal Communication" (Chapter 1, Introduction) explains: There is a natural ordering of daily decisions. What does §2.1 (The ISO Container) of Container Vessel Stowage Planning explain?

Section 2.1 "The ISO Container" (Chapter 2, Containers) explains: Containers are often referred to as boxes or units by stowage planners. An ISO container is defined according to the following ISO standards: 668, 830, 1161, 6346, 1496-(1-5) [25]. It is a boxed formed transport equipment strong enough to be used repeatedly. The container is designed to be used for multiple modes of transportation and it should be easy to fill and empty.

What does §2.1.2 (Structure and Strength) of Container Vessel Stowage Planning explain?

Section 2.1.2 "Structure and Strength" (Chapter 2, Containers) explains: ISO containers are composed of a steal framework, which is their weight bearing element. As shown in Figure 2.2, the framework is composed of two bottom and two top side rails. The floor is supported by a number of cross members, and the roof by a top cross member and a door header. The walls, roof, and the door are the weakest parts of the container structure and are not able to bear any considerable weight. Section 2.2.3 "Flat Rack Containers" (Chapter 2, Containers) explains: Flat racks consist of a floor structure with a high loading capacity composed of a steel frame and a softwood floor and two end walls, which may either be fixed or collapsible. What does §2.2.10 (Tank Containers (TK)) of Container Vessel Stowage Planning explain?

What does §2.2.11 (Dry Bulk Containers) of Container Vessel Stowage Planning explain?

Section 2.2.11 "Dry Bulk Containers" (Chapter 2, Containers) explains: Aside from tanks, dry bulk like grain and fertilizer can be transported in dry bulk containers, where the cargo will not be pressurized. There are two kinds of dry bulk containers: box type, equipped with doors in one of the end walls which allows the containers to be use for general purpose cargo, and hopper type intended for horizontal discharge and without any doors. Both container types must be equipped with suitable cargo loading opening and must be weather-proof. The internal constructions of the container allows for full discharge of the cargo. Section 5.1 "Terminal Layout and Equipment" (Chapter 5, Container Terminals) explains: Acontainer terminal is an inter-modal hub for the shipment of containers.
